summary: DeVault introduces hare-wren, a Hare language binding for the Wren scripting language, as an alternative to embedding Lua. He evaluated Wren as a lightweight embeddable scripting language and found it surprisingly capable despite minor complaints about scope rules and its CLI standard library. The binding exposes Wren's C API through idiomatic Hare interfaces, supports bidirectional function calls between Hare and Wren, and includes an optional async runtime built on hare-ev. He also implemented a replacement standard library for Wren that improves on Wren CLI's design.
key_points: Wren was chosen over Lua as an embeddable scripting language for Hare, offering a surprisingly capable feature set for its simplicity
The hare-wren binding reshapes Wren's C API into idiomatic Hare rather than exposing the C API directly
An optional wren::api submodule provides an async runtime based on hare-ev and a reimagined standard library that intentionally diverges from Wren CLI
The project includes an hwren command-line tool analogous to wren-cli but without REPL support
money_quotes: I found that Wren punches far above its weight for such a simple language.
I elected to mold it into a more idiomatic Hare interface rather than expose the C API directly to Hare.
I felt that the Wren CLI libraries had a lot of room for improvement, so I made the call to implement a standard library which is only somewhat compatible with Wren CLI.
Its scope rules are silly, the C API has some odd limitations here and there, and in my opinion the "standard library" provided by wren CLI is poorly designed.
Surprisingly, my list of complaints more or less ends there, and I was excited to build a nice interface to it from Hare.
key_insight: Wren offers a compelling alternative to Lua for embedding a scripting language in systems programs, and hare-wren demonstrates how to build idiomatic language bindings with an improved standard library on top.
